stem: When is a plain sliding window the RIGHT context strategy?
options: When conversations regularly reference decisions made much earlier
When production logs show older messages are rarely referenced and users can easily re-state what's needed
When exact numbers must survive the whole session
When safety-critical facts like allergies are involved
rationales: Referring back to earlier decisions is exactly where sliding windows fail.
Simple and cheap wins when the traffic profile supports it — e.g., 94% of messages referencing only the last 3–5 exchanges. The rare reach-back can be handled by asking the user to re-state.
Exact facts need structured state or retrieval, not truncation.
Safety-critical facts must survive verbatim in a retained reference section.

stem: A RAG-backed assistant accumulates retrieval results from many earlier queries, crowding out conversational coherence. What is the recommended fix?
options: Summarize all retrievals into one running digest
Aggressively deduplicate retrieved passages before injection
Move all retrievals to a vector store and re-retrieve per turn
Apply a sliding window specifically to RAG results (keep the last 2–3 retrievals) while conversation history keeps its own policy
rationales: A mega-digest is more complicated and rarely better — and it blurs the specific passages the current question needs.
Deduplication helps at the margin but doesn't stop unbounded accumulation.
Re-retrieving everything each turn adds infrastructure to solve what a simple windowing policy solves directly.
Different content types deserve different retention policies. Old retrievals are usually stale; recent ones matter. Windowing RAG separately preserves conversation flow.

stem: What distinguishes a USEFUL progressive summary from a bad one?
options: Useful summaries are shorter
Useful summaries are written by a separate summarization model
Useful summaries are structured — decisions, current preferences, open questions, important facts — while bad ones are vague narrative prose that loses the exact details users later ask about
Useful summaries include every message verbatim
rationales: Brevity without structure just loses information faster.
Who writes it matters less than what it captures.
The failure mode of summarization is blurring specifics. Explicitly extracting decisions, preferences, open questions, and facts preserves what "describe the conversation" prose loses.
That's not a summary — it defeats the purpose.

stem: A cooking assistant must remember "room temperature butter means 68°F in this kitchen" and a shellfish allergy for the entire session, while ordinary chat is summarized freely. Where do those exact facts belong?
options: In the most recent turns, repeated periodically by the assistant
In a retained reference section at the start of context, exempt from trimming and summarization
In the progressive summary, marked as important
In an external database queried when the topic comes up
rationales: Recent turns roll off; repetition depends on the model remembering to repeat.
Content that must stay exact and stable gets its own protected section — trimming applies only to the surrounding discussion. Mixing them into one summarization pass is how allergies get blurred.
Summaries are exactly where exact details go to die, "important" label or not.
Retrieval can miss; safety-critical session facts are small enough to keep permanently in context.

stem: Users revise preferences mid-session ("actually, make the budget $4,200"), and the assistant sometimes applies outdated ones. What is the most reliable fix?
options: A system prompt instruction: "always prioritize the most recently stated preferences"
Few-shot examples of correctly applying preference changes
Pruning turns that contain superseded preferences
A canonical structured state object updated on every revision and included in each request — a single source of current truth
rationales: The model usually follows it — but not reliably enough, since old and new values still compete in context.
Examples improve framing but don't give the model one authoritative place to look.
Pruning can delete context needed for other reasons, and revision turns are easy to miss.
Don't make the model infer current truth from a history containing both old and new values. Maintain the truth explicitly; send it every turn.

stem: A user says "I have very low risk tolerance" and later "I want to maximize returns like my friends did with crypto." What should the assistant do?
options: Follow the more recent statement — recency wins
Recommend a balanced middle-ground portfolio
Surface the contradiction and ask which priority should govern
Follow the first statement — safety-related preferences take precedence
rationales: Recency resolves revisions, not contradictions the user hasn't noticed making.
A balanced compromise risks fitting neither stated preference — the classic hidden-compromise failure.
Incompatible goals are the user's decision to resolve. Name the tension explicitly; proceed once one priority governs.
Assuming precedence silently still guesses — and this decision matters.

stem: A research assistant summarizes paper discussions after 8 turns, but users then ask for precise p-values and sample sizes the summaries blurred. What is the most direct fix?
options: Re-inject the relevant source sections on demand when a question signals the need for precision
Build a structured fact store of every numerical detail in every paper
Make the summaries higher-fidelity so they preserve all numbers
Stop summarizing and keep full transcripts
rationales: On-demand retrieval restores exactness only when needed and scales across unpredictable follow-ups.
Heavier to build and may not match the variety of questions users actually ask.
Summaries that preserve every number balloon back toward the original document.
That reintroduces the context growth the summarization existed to control.

stem: An agent investigating return requests has called `lookup_order` many times; each response has 40+ fields and the raw outputs now dominate context. What is the most reliable approach?
options: Summarize all the order responses into one prose paragraph
Move the responses to a vector database and retrieve as needed
Compress each prior response to its return-relevant fields (order_id, dates, items, return_window, status) and continue
Stop making lookups and work from what's already in context
rationales: Prose summarization blurs the IDs and dates the investigation turns on.
Retrieval infrastructure to re-find data the agent already has is complexity without benefit.
Tool result compression: after processing, keep the fields that matter for the task and drop backend noise. Context stays lean without losing decision-relevant facts.
The investigation may genuinely need more lookups; the fix is compressing results, not starving the task.

stem: How do API-native compaction and context editing differ, and when is application-level management still required?
options: Compaction deletes old turns; context editing rewrites them — use either when facts must survive verbatim

Compaction summarizes earlier history into a compact block; context editing prunes stale content like old tool results — but when specific facts must survive verbatim, application-level structures (state objects, reference sections) are still the right tool
They are the same mechanism with different names
Both require you to build and tune your own summarization pipeline
rationales: Backwards: compaction summarizes rather than deletes, and neither guarantees verbatim survival of specific facts.

Summarize vs prune is the distinction. API-native mechanisms excel at "keep a long session alive" with no plumbing; application-level control is for content you must guarantee survives. They compose well.
They do different work — one condenses, one clears.
Avoiding that pipeline is precisely their value.

stem: A customer returns 6 hours after a support session about a pending billing case. How should the new session begin?
options: Resume the full prior transcript with an instruction to prefer recent tool results
Resume the transcript but filter out old tool_result messages
Configure the agent to re-call all previously used tools at session start
Start with a structured summary of prior interaction (issue, actions taken, known IDs, last known status) and fetch fresh state before making claims about current status
rationales: Agents reference old tool results regardless of instructions — especially when the stale results are more detailed than fresh ones.
Filtering results while keeping turns that reference them confuses the model about missing data.
Re-calling everything wastes calls on tools irrelevant to the new question.
Tool results age. A compact structured summary carries continuity; targeted fresh lookups carry truth. `fresh_lookup_required: true` makes staleness explicit.

stem: Mid-conversation, an external system learns the user's order has shipped. The user hasn't asked about it. How does this information reach the model?
options: Your application includes the fresh state in the next request — a state block or context section — made clearly more authoritative than any stale tool results
Claude notices the change through its connection to your order system
Generate an unsolicited assistant message announcing the shipment
Wait until the user asks, then have a tool look it up
rationales: The model knows nothing outside the request. External updates enter as application-injected state — and must outrank older tool results that say otherwise.
No such connection exists; the API is stateless.
Unsolicited assistant turns are a product decision, not a context mechanism — don't fabricate them unless proactive notification is intentional.
Viable, but it leaves the model asserting stale status if the user asks indirectly; injecting current state is the principled fix.

stem: A model has a very large context window, so the team stops worrying about what goes into context. What are they confusing?
options: Input pricing with output pricing
Latency with throughput
Capacity with attention — fitting in the window doesn't make every detail equally salient
Context windows with training data size
rationales: Pricing isn't the conceptual error here.
Neither is the latency/throughput distinction.
A huge window means content *can* be included, not that it will be *attended to* equally. Curation still matters — competing, stale, and verbose content dilutes what's important.
Training data is unrelated to per-request context.
